explanation:
The natural language specification states that each Data Manager (DM) must transmit its local matrix instance to other DMs every period (every 1 time unit). It also implies the system must be able to receive and process up to 49 matrices in that time. However, the atomic propositions provided focus primarily on individual matrix updates being sent and received between pairs of DMs, as well as DM1 performing updates.

Given these atomic propositions, to encode that every DM should periodically send updates to all others, the closest expressible property is that for each possible matrix update send action (from DM1 to DM2... to DM5), this event should happen infinitely often (i.e., eventually, again and again), representing the periodic transmission. Thus, we can specify that globally, eventually each matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dmX occurs, for each possible destination, meaning that in every time period, each transmission will occur at least once.

The original sentence also mentioned the ability to receive and process up to 49 matrices within 1 time unit, but there is no atomic proposition that collectively counts or limits this; therefore, it's not possible to encode this aspect without numerical comparison operators or counting constructs. Similarly, messages for DUs updating their navigation data rely on matrix updates occurring from DM to DM (as represented by sends and receives) and are not directly encoded in the given atomic propositions.

Thus, this LTL formula expresses the periodic guarantee that each required transmission occurs infinitely often, which simulates the 1-periodic update cycle as best as possible with the atomic propositions provided.
output_LTL: G (F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)

explanation:
The natural language requirement specifies that each DM (Distributed Module) should transmit its local matrix instance to other DMs with a period of 1 time unit, and each DM should be able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit. Based on the provided atomic propositions, the closest relevant atomic propositions are 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2', 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3', etc., representing the sending of matrix updates between DMs, and 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2', etc., representing their reception. 

Since the requirement is periodic communication (every time unit), this can be modeled by requiring that globally (at every time step), a matrix update is sent from each DM to each of the others. Given the available atomic propositions, and focusing on DM1 as representative, we require that in every state, DM1 sends matrix updates to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. This is expressed with the 'G' (globally) operator:

G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)

This formula captures that at every time unit, DM1 sends its matrix to all other DMs—which is the periodic update transmission required. It uses only the available atomic propositions and LTL operators. If we had generic propositions for any DM, we could generalize further, but with the given set, we illustrate with the DM1-specific atoms.

Previous LTL properties, if any, are not provided. This property directly and faithfully encodes the specified requirement as far as possible with the provided atoms.

The input natural language specifies that matrix updates must occur such that every DM can update navigation data in every time unit (second), each DM both sends out its matrix to others and is able to receive up to 49 matrices in the same period. The available atomic propositions are limited in referring specifically to transmissions from DM1 to other DMs, as well as generic update sending/receiving actions. To express that every DM sends its matrix to each other every time unit, we use the available atomic propositions for DM1 transmissions (as a representative example). The property must ensure that globally, in every time unit, each matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 and that DM1 is able to receive updates from other DMs in every time unit. Given that the atomic propositions do not allow modeling of the receiving constraint up to 49 matrices (no counting in LTL), we focus on the periodic updates.

So, we use the 'G[0:1]' operator to assert that, at every time instant and the next, all matrix updates from DM1 to other DMs (and vice versa, where possible) occur. This partially captures the update condition (since the atomic propositions only provide update actions between DM1 and other DMs), asserting that matrix updates between DMs must occur in every time unit, as required by the specification. However, the exact value '49' cannot be expressed because counting is not available in standard LTL and no corresponding atomic proposition is provided.
output_LTL: G[0:1] (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

explanation: The natural language statement specifies that every DM must transmit its local matrix to the others every time unit, with each DM able to receive and process updates within that interval. As the atomic propositions reference specific DM-to-DM transmissions (such as 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2') and receptions (such as 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2'), we ensure that, for each pair of DMs, an update is eventually sent and then, within the same time unit, received by the others. This can be represented by stating that globally, every time a clock ticks, all relevant matrix updates are sent and received. Since LTL does not natively describe bounded message rates or queues, the property captures that, upon each clock occurrence, matrix updates are both sent and received between DMs, matching the periodicity and synchronicity requirements implied in the input.
output_LTL: G (clock_occurred -> (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))

explanation:
The natural language specifies that each DM transmits its local matrix to the other DMs every time unit, and each DM is able to receive and process up to 49 matrices in a time unit. Since we must only use provided atomic propositions and not numerical comparison operators, the most corresponding LTL property expressible is that, globally, whenever a clock tick occurs, each DM eventually (before the next clock tick) sends its matrix update to the other DMs and each DM eventually receives the update from the others. Based on the atomic propositions, we capture the sending and receiving events related to DM1 (since atomic propositions for all DMs are not available, but those for DM1's interactions are). Thus, the LTL property below states that globally, after every clock_occurred, eventually in the next period, all matrix updates from DM1 are sent to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5, and that DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. This models, as closely as expressible with available atomic propositions, the specified requirements for matrix update frequency and exchange.

No previously given LTL properties are present. If we tried to encode specific bounds on the number of updates (e.g., 49 per time unit), this would violate the prohibition of numerical comparisons and would not be possible with the atomic propositions provided. Therefore, the given formula is the most precise translation in permitted LTL syntax.

For DM1:
G( clock_occurred -> (F[0:1] (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & F[0:1] (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5) ) )

Here, F[0:1] p = p | Xp, which expresses 'eventually within one step'.
output_LTL: G( clock_occurred -> ( (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 | X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5))  & (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5 | X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)) ) )

explanation: The natural language states that each DM must send its local matrix instance to the others every time unit, so that every DU can update its navigation data every second. It also says a DM can receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices each time unit. The closest atomic propositions relate to sending and receiving matrix updates between DMs, as well as a 'clock_occurred' event. Since there is no atomic proposition capturing all 49 receptions directly, the most faithful translation is that for every clock tick, each DM must send the update to the other DMs, and each DM must receive updates from all the others within the same period. As an example, for DM1: 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2',..., 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5' and 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2',..., etc. must always happen at every clock occurrence. This is captured by using the 'G[0:1]' operator to say that at every instant and its next, these send and receive actions happen (since G[0:1]p is equivalent to p and next p, it captures that the property is enforced in every time step repeatedly).
output_LTL: G[0:1] (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

explanation:
The input specifies a real-time periodicity requirement: each DM must send its local matrix to all other DMs every period (1 time unit), ensuring that every DM can update navigation data each second (i.e., each time unit). Since LTL lacks native timing and numeric comparison, but relies on recurring patterns, we interpret 'period of 1 time unit' as 'globally, for every time step, a matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5', and similarly, each DM receives a new matrix from every other DM in each time step. Since the atomic propositions only specify named communications and updates, we capture the cycling requirement as: globally, in every next step, each of these send and receive events happens (without gaps). The formula uses G[0:1] for strict periodicity (LTL approximation without numeric timing).

The previously given atomic propositions do not directly represent the ability to "elaborate 49 matrices in 1 unit", so we focus on ensuring that each send and receive happens in every step. This captures the core intent of the specification within the limitations of discrete LTL and provided atomic propositions.

explanation: The natural language requirement describes the need for each DM (Distributed Module) to transmit its local instance of matrix M to the other DMs every time unit, ensuring that each DM can update its navigation data every second, and be able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in one time period. Since the atomic propositions provided refer to specific matrix update events between pairs of DMs, as well as the generic sending and receiving of local matrices, but do not express counts or frequencies numerically, we express the requirement that every matrix update sent from a DM to another DM eventually leads to the receiving DM performing an update. This is captured by the following property, which for simplicity, we illustrate for DM1 receiving updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. We use the 'Globally' operator to indicate this must always hold and the 'eventually' operator to guarantee the consequence always happens after the trigger. This does not explicitly encode the numeric bounds (such as 1 second or 49 matrices) due to the limitation to atomic propositions and temporal logic operators only, but it encodes the essential periodic and coverage guarantees implied by the requirement.

explanation:
Given the new restriction to only use the provided atomic propositions, and considering the requirement that each DM must transmit its local matrix to the others every time unit (approximating a frequency of 1 per time unit), we focus on the atomic propositions for sending and receiving matrix updates between specific DMs, the 'clock_occurred' tick, and 'dm1_performs_update'.

There are matrix update send and receive propositions for DM1 with DM2-DM5 (either as the sender or receiver). The best direct way to express periodic communication by DM1 is to require that, globally, whenever a clock tick occurs, DM1 sends matrix updates to all other DMs (DM2—DM5). Similarly, we require that, globally, after such a send has occurred, the corresponding DM1 receives the matrix update from the other DMs in the future (since matrix updates could also be received by DM1). We also include the update action by DM1 itself to capture matrix processing.

This does not exactly express a frequency or "49 matrices in 1 time unit", but, given only the available atomic propositions and LTL's limitations (no numerical counting or direct time periods), this captures that on every clock tick, all possible DM1 matrix interactions with other DMs should occur: sending updates and eventually receiving updates. This structure best satisfies the periodic update and communication aspect of the requirement, as precise counts cannot be enforced without numerical operators or explicit buffering propositions.
output_LTL: G (clock_occurred -> (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5 & F dm1_performs_update))

To best approximate this, an LTL property can require that, globally (G), 'every time a clock occurs (clock_occurred), eventually (F) a matrix update is both sent and received between DMs.' Because the input asks for a guarantee every second, using G(clock_occurred -> F(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)) expresses that each time the clock ticks, all updates to and from DM1 must eventually occur.
